figures.caption: Table 1. Performance on LiveCodeBench v5 and v6. We compare our RefineRL models (4B and 4B-2507 versions) against baselines ranging from 4B to 235B parameters. By applying our Skeptical-Agent (i.e., Skeptical@16) to RefineRL-4B-2507, our approach outperforms 32B baselines and achieves performance comparable to the single-attempt performance of significantly larger 235B model.

Table 6. Statistics of the training dataset for Self-Refinement RL. This table details the sample counts for each interaction category. “WA/RE/TLE/CE/MLE” represent trajectories triggered by error-driven feedback. “AC → WA/RE/TLE/CE/MLE” denote trajectories involving skepticism-driven feedback, where the solution passed public test cases but failed on private ones due to the indicated errors.
Figure 6. Detailed templates for constructing the “Error Reason” field based on specific execution errors (WA, TLE, RE, etc.).

Table 2. Performance of inference-time strategies. We compare our Skeptical-Agent (Skeptical@16) strategy with different inference-time baselines on 4B models, both before and after self-refinement RL. Notably, our Skeptical-Agent (i.e., Skeptical@16) consistently outperforms the compared baselines, achieving the best inference-time performance across all settings.
Figure 4. The standard system prompt used for initial solution generation and subsequent refinement steps.
Figure 5. Prompt for error-driven feedback construction.

Figure 1. Overview of our RefineRL approach. (a) Skeptical-Agent employs local tools to evaluate generated solutions against visible public test cases and enforces rigorous self-refinement even when evaluation results suggest correctness. (b) Self-Refinement RL utilizes self-generated refinement trajectory data from the Skeptical-Agent on real-world CP problems data, along with a novel Squared-incentive reward function, to advance the self-refinement capabilities of LLMs.

Figure 2. Training dynamics of Qwen3-4B. The left plot shows the steady increase in reward, while the right plot shows the corresponding growth in response length, reflecting the model’s internalization of the skeptical reasoning process.

Figure 3. Training dynamics of Qwen3-4B-2507. Similar to the base model, the 2507 variant demonstrates consistent learning progress in terms of reward maximization and increased reasoning length during the RL process.
Table 5. Performance of models trained via Skeptical-RL with the original squared-incentive reward or pass-or-fail reward.

Table 7. Comparison of RL Training Strategies. We contrast three RL setups: Vanilla RL (direct generation), Self-Refinement RL with pass-or-fail rewards, and our Self-Refinement RL (with squared-incentive rewards). Vanilla RL uses only the initial generation subset (∼4.5k), while refinement methods use the full trajectory dataset (∼8.7k). Our method employs a squared reward to incentivize robust self-verification.
Figure 7. The skeptical-driven feedback prompt (PSkep) triggered when a solution passes local tests (on all public test cases), instructing the agent to self-critique against a checklist.
